title: Moire-Engineered Excitonic Landscape and Phonon-Mediated Recombination in Twisted WSe2 Bilayers
authors: Memansa Thapa, Aksa Thomas, Jayalekshmi U. J., Krishna Prasad Bera, Darshit Solanki, Kenji Watanabe, Takashi Taniguchi, Ajay Kumar Shukla, Anindya Das, Ajay Soni
abstract: We report light emission from the moire superlattice of a twisted bilayer of tungsten diselenide (WSe2/WSe2) encapsulated in insulating hexagonal boron nitride (hBN). The low-temperature photoluminescence (PL) spectroscopy reveals signatures of moire-potential induced strong interlayer excitonic emission and phonon-assisted recombination, while the twisting significantly suppresses the emission from localized defect-bound excitons. The moire potential redistributes carriers into indirect valleys, thereby enhancing recombination efficiency and stabilizing the interlayer excitons. Our findings establish that precise control of twist angle and dielectric environment provides a new route for engineering excitonic systems for exploring exciton-phonon interactions and associated quantum phenomena in transition metal dichalcogenides.
summary: This work reports on the use of moiré engineering in twisted WSe2 bilayers to control optoelectronic properties. By tuning the twist angle, researchers observe enhanced interlayer exciton emission and strong exciton-phonon coupling. This establishes the TBL as a highly tunable platform for studying quantum phenomena in 2D materials.
main_problem: The study investigates how engineering the twist angle in twisted WSe2 bilayers modifies the excitonic landscape and controls phonon-mediated recombination processes.
main_result: The moiré potential significantly enhances interlayer exciton emission and stabilizes these excitons by redistributing carriers into indirect valleys, while also suppressing defect-related emission.
method: The research employs low-temperature photoluminescence (PL) spectroscopy and temperature-dependent Raman spectroscopy to probe electronic and vibrational properties.
model_or_system: The physical system is a twisted bilayer of tungsten diselenide (WSe2/WSe2) encapsulated in hBN, forming a moiré superlattice. The analysis models temperature dependence using the Varshni and O’Donnell equations.
key_observables: Interlayer exciton emission ($	ext{X}_{	ext{I}}$), phonon replicas ($	ext{X}_{	ext{p}1	ext{I}}, 	ext{X}_{	ext{p}2	ext{I}}$), PL intensity, and Raman mode splittings.
important_parameters: Twist angle ($	heta$), temperature (3 K to 300 K), and fitting parameters like Varshni ($\alpha,  eta$) and O’Donnell ($S, \hbar\omega$) constants.
main_assumptions: The electronic structure modification is assumed to be dictated by the moiré potential, and temperature effects are modeled using established empirical equations (Varshni/O’Donnell).
figures_summary: Figures show PL spectra comparing different stacking orders (ML, NBL, TBL) and Raman spectra demonstrating mode splitting due to moiré potential hybridization.
paper_structure: The paper systematically compares PL and Raman signatures across various stacking configurations (ML, NBL, ABL, TBL) to quantify how twist angle controls exciton localization, phonon coupling, and temperature-dependent bandgap renormalization.
why_interesting: The detailed study of exciton-phonon coupling in a tunable, engineered quantum material platform (TBL) provides quantitative parameters for understanding open quantum systems dynamics in 2D semiconductors.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: Figure 1. Structural configuration of twisted bilayers. (a) Top-view of the crystal structure of 2H-WSe2. (b) Schematic of twisting of bilayer WSe2. (c) Schematic representation of the moiré superlattice resulting from the twisting of two WSe2 monolayers, with insets showing the various atomic stackings AA, AB, and SP (saddle point). (d) The Brillouin zones of top (green) and

Figure 2. Raman spectra at 3 K for various stackings of WSe2 layers. Raman spectra of monolayer (ML), natural bilayer (NBL), and fabricated bilayers with twist angle, θ ∼ 60º (artificial bilayer, ABL) and θ ∼ 2º (twisted bilayer, TBL). The insets illustrate the schematics of atomic displacements of the A1g, E2g and B2g modes, where blue and purple spheres denote W and Se atoms, respectively. The intensity of high frequency modes (370 - 425 cm-1) is amplified ~ 5 times for better visibility.

Figure 3. Normalized PL spectra at 3 K for various stacking layers of WSe2. (a) Monolayer (ML), (b) natural bilayer (NBL), (c) artificial bilayer fabricated with twist angle, θ ∼ 60º (ABL), and (d) twisted bilayer with θ ∼ 2º (TBL).

Figure 4. Temperature dependence of PL for TBL. (a) Normalized PL spectra of TBL between 3 K to 300 K. Temperature-dependent shifting of (b) neutral exciton (X0), (c) trion (X−), and (d) interlayer exciton (XI), with solid lines representing the O’Donnell equation fit.[8,59]

Figure 4a shows the evolution of the PL spectrum as a function of temperature in the range 3 K to 300 K. Notably, within the temperature range 3 K - 50 K, the phonon replicas are well resolved and prominent, signifying strong exciton-phonon coupling.
